What if the order isn’t specified?
Given:      k+ ; l+; m+  	x	 kk; ll; mm
Given a test cross with a triple heterozygote, one can tell the arrangement of genes on the beginning chromosomes.

Given:			k+ ; l+; m+  	x	 kk; ll; mm
Possible Orders: 	      +++	+++	        +++
klm	kml             mkl

+            +             +          +           l            +
k             l              m         k           +           m
+            +             +          +            k            +
m           k           l         m           +           l
+             +             +          +            m            +
k             m           l           k            +              l
3 different models yield 3 different double recombination results
If this order is right              you get these double recombinants

klm	kml             mkl

Observed Double Crossovers Match Only 1 Possible Gene Order

So, if order is not specified:
Establish which are the parental chromosomes
Build three models of the parental chromosomes by varying the gene in the middle
Do a double recombination for each model (if the order is this, then the recombinant would be that)
Look at your numbers, the double recombinant should be the smallest class.  See which model fits the data and choose that order.
Then use your correct model to proceed with the distance mapping

kml

Reg I 	         DCO
(103+109) + (7+3)= 222/1572=	0.141
Reg II	DCO
(64+57) + (7+3)	=131/1572=0.083
Region I                Region II
k                                            m         l
14.8 cM                             8.3 cM

Summary of steps for determining linkage order and map distances
1)	Look at the cross, and at the offspring
2)	Establish which class of the offspring is the parentals, and the double crossovers
3)	If the order of genes is not specified, establish the correct linear order of the genes by model building
Group the single crossovers into regions
5)	Determine distances,  being sure to include the double crossovers in calculations
